text: This is a brief video of a robotic right hepatectomy with a technical description. I will take you through the key steps of this operation, which include extrahepatic inflow control, the retrohepatic cavell dissection, hepatic parenchymal division with the laparoscopic CUSA, and the intraparenchymal hepatic vein outflow control. Our patient is a forty-year-old otherwise healthy woman with a large right liver mass. MRI with EOVIST was consistent with a liver adenoma, and the mass involved segments five, six, seven, and eight, so she has consented for a robotic right hepatectomy.

Like any other minimally invasive surgery, patient positioning and port placement are critical to the success of the operation. This is a diagram of my port placement for robotic hepatectomy. The patient is supine with a bump under the right abdomen and is placed in ten to fifteen degrees of reverse Trendelenburg position. The black dots on the diagram indicate the robotic ports and the assist ports are shown with the blue dots. The laparoscopic CUZA is used through the assist port located at the right subcostal midclavicular line. Specimen extraction is typically through a fan and steel incision or extension of the umbilical port site.

The surgeon begins the hilar dissection with a cholecystectomy, dissecting, clipping, and dividing the cystic duct and artery, and lowering the hilar plate. An endo loop encircles the cystic duct stump to retract the bile duct medially, exposing the posterior lateral liver hilum. The right hepatic artery is identified, circumferentially dissected, occluded, and its patency confirmed with Doppler ultrasound. The artery is tied with a silk tie, clipped, and divided, revealing the right portal vein. The portal vein is circumferentially dissected, with branches to segment six and caudate branches isolated and divided. The portal vein is tied, clipped with endoclips, and divided, completing the hilar dissection and extrahepatic inflow control.

The surgeon performs retrohepatic caval dissection, separating the retrohepatic inferior vena cava from the liver by dividing multiple short hepatic veins using hemlock clips or a vessel sealer. A larger inferior right hepatic vein is tied, clipped, and divided, continuing the dissection until the base of the right hepatic vein is visible.

The surgeon marks the planned parenchymal division plane, preserving the middle hepatic vein identified with ultrasound. Indocyanine green is administered, showing demarcation with the left hepatic lobe uptaking the dye while the right lobe remains dark. Parenchymal transection is performed using the laparoscopic CUSA and robotic vessel sealer, achieving a bloodless dissection. Intrahepatic vascular and biliary structures are dissected and controlled. The right hepatic duct is divided intraparenchymally, avoiding the hepatic duct bifurcation. The right hilar plate with the hepatic duct is encircled and divided using a robotic stapler with a vascular staple load. The right hepatic vein is dissected intraparenchymally and stapled, completing the parenchymal division.

The surgical specimen is placed in a large endobag and extracted through a fan and steel incision. The liver remnant is inspected, showing good vascularization. Hemostasis is verified, and the left hepatic artery and left portal vein inflow are confirmed using Doppler ultrasound.

In follow-up clinic at one week she was on minimal pain medication without any narcotic requirements. I hope you enjoyed this video of a robotic right hepatectomy. Please subscribe to my YouTube channel and have a great day.
